title: US manufacturing employment is down, but each state has its own story
gist: US manufacturing employment fell 0.4% year-over-year to May 2026, but that national figure masks wide dispersion: twelve states grew, three were flat, and the rest declined, with small states swinging hardest.

insights.claim: US manufacturing employment decreased 0.4% year-over-year from May 2025 to May 2026.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: A sub-1% national contraction confirms manufacturing labor is stagnant-to-shrinking, a soft signal for industrial demand and capex-sensitive plays.
insights.quote: From last May to this May, manufacturing employment decreased by 0.4%.
insights.locator: From last May to this May

themes.theme: State-level dispersion beneath the national number

insights.claim: Beneath the national manufacturing decline, twelve states grew, three were flat, and the rest declined as of May 2026.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: A single national headline hides tradeable regional divergence; investors tracking industrial or regional exposure should look at state-level series, not the aggregate.
insights.locator: Most states are in negative territory

insights.claim: In small states or those with a limited manufacturing base, a single factory opening or closing can drive large percentage swings in manufacturing employment.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Extreme state-level percentage moves are often idiosyncratic single-plant events, not trend signals, so they should be read with caution.
insights.quote: a factory or two opening or closing can have a big impact, especially if the state is small or doesn’t have a large manufacturing base to begin with

insights.claim: The steepest year-over-year manufacturing employment declines were in the District of Columbia (-9.1%), Alaska (-5.6%), Virginia (-4.9%), and Nebraska (-4.6%), while the strongest gains were in Connecticut (+3%), Louisiana (+2%), and Rhode Island (+1.5%).
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: The named outliers flag which state economies are gaining or losing factory jobs fastest, useful for regional labor and industrial exposure screening.
